summary: Gruber examines the limitations of AirPlay video streaming in the newly released iOS 4.2, finding that it only works in the built-in iPod/Video app and YouTube, while being audio-only everywhere else. He highlights the glaring omission that videos shot on the iPhone can't be played back on Apple TV via AirPlay, despite still photos working fine. He investigates and debunks the theory that encoding bandwidth is the bottleneck, demonstrating that the same iPhone-shot video plays over AirPlay when synced through iTunes. He concludes Apple likely cut broader AirPlay video support to hit the November shipping deadline for iOS 4.2.
key_points: AirPlay video in iOS 4.2 only works from the built-in iPod/Video app and YouTube — all other apps are limited to audio-only streaming
The most obvious missing use case is playing iPhone-shot video on a TV via AirPlay, which doesn't work despite still photos streaming fine
The encoding bandwidth theory doesn't hold up: the same iPhone video file streams over AirPlay from the iPod app after being synced through iTunes
AirPlay video doesn't work from Mobile Safari either, suggesting the limitation is systemic rather than content-specific
Apple likely shipped iOS 4.2 with incomplete AirPlay video support to meet a November deadline, particularly for iPad users
money_quotes: AirPlay is the killer feature for Apple TV. But perhaps we'd better keep that in the future tense for now: it's going to be the killer feature for Apple TV.
Shoot a video on your iPhone, then play it back for family and friends on your big TV via AirPlay. No syncing, no docking, no moving files around. Just hit play. But it doesn't work in iOS 4.2.1.
The same video streams over AirPlay from one app (iPad/Video), but not another (Photos).
So my best guess is that Apple really wanted to ship iOS 4.2 in November — especially for the sake of the iPad — and some features didn't make the cut.
key_insight: AirPlay video's per-app limitations in iOS 4.2 aren't a technical constraint but a shipping deadline compromise, as proven by the same video file streaming fine from one app but not another.
